<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高安のビジュン]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高安のビジュン]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高安のビジュン]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q28685432]] Wikidata name: Bijun of Takayasu [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 09:45, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Takayasu no Bijun''' (高安のビジュン) is a worship site dedicated to a sacred stone located in Takayasu, [[:en:Tomigusuku, Okinawa|Tomigusuku]], Okinawa Prefecture.
== Overview == <ref>Tomigusuku City Board of Education Cultural Affairs Division, "Takayasu no Bijun Cultural Property Information Board," Tomigusuku City Board of Education, 2015.</ref> * ''Bijuru'' or ''Bijun'' refers to a tradition of worshiping sacred stones, primarily found throughout the main island of Okinawa. In Tomigusuku, it is commonly known as Bijun. At Takayasu no Bijun, a single rounded sandstone called ''Nībinu-funi'' (fine-grained sandstone) is enshrined as the sacred stone.
* The following legend is associated with this Bijun: "Long ago, two youths from {{ill|Kanegusuku Magiri|ja|兼城間切|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanegusuku Magiri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Zaha (present-day Itoman City Zaha) were each carrying sacred sake intended as an offering to the king in Shuri. They stopped to rest in front of the Bijun. Tired from carrying the heavy sake, each made a request to the Bijun. One youth wished, 'Please make my sake lighter,' while the other earnestly asked, 'Please give me the strength to carry this sake.' The youth who lazily wished for lighter sake had the rope holding his sake barrel snap, spilling all of the sake. Meanwhile, the sincere youth who asked for strength safely delivered the sake to Shuri. Thus, the Bijun deity is revered as a benevolent god who grants strength to the earnest." This story is known as the Takayasu no Bijun Monogatari and was adapted into a kamishibai by the Takayasu Children's Association.<ref>{{cite book|language=ja|author1=Tomigusuku Minwa no Kai|author2=Kimio Isa|title=Takayasu Bijun Monogatari|year=1997}}</ref>
* At Takayasu no Bijun, a traditional event called Bijun Hēshi (also known as Agī Hēshi) is held annually on the 13th day of the 9th month of the lunar calendar.<ref name="city">{{cite book|language=ja|author=Tomigusuku City History Editing Committee, Folk Culture Division|title=Tomigusuku City History, Volume 2: Folk Culture|publisher=Tomigusuku City Hall|year=2008|pages=616,628}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|language=ja|author=Takayasu District History Editing Committee|title=Takayasu-shi Vol.1|year=1999|page=61}}</ref> Until around 1960, many local villagers gathered at Bijun square for festive singing accompanied by the [[:en:Sanshin|sanshin]]. Nowadays, families individually visit the shrine to pray for good health and household safety, and it has become customary for households celebrating new births or new homes to offer bottles of [[:en:Awamori|awamori]].<ref name="city"/> Additionally, younger village officials called ''yakume'' clean the area around Bijun the day before, and on the day of the event, they gather coral stones from Senaga Island as offerings to welcome visitors.
* Other customs include village officials worshipping at the "Umanchū" shrine on {{ill|Senaga Island|ja|瀬長島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senaga Island|1=Senaga Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4518914}} the day before Bijun Hēshi until before WWII, and even today, on the following day (the 14th), clan groups ({{ill|munchū|ja|門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/munchū|1=munchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10333146}}) perform a ceremony called Shinagamunumē (also known as Anjinamunumē) on Senaga Island.<ref>{{cite book|language=ja|author=Tomigusuku Village Board of Education|title=Cultural Properties of Tomigusuku Village (Revised)|year=2002|page=13}}</ref> The exact historical relationship between Takayasu and Senaga Island is unclear, but the Bijun Hēshi period is a significant time for interaction between the people of Takayasu and Senaga Island.
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * Tomigusuku City Board of Education Cultural Affairs Division, "Takayasu no Bijun Cultural Property Information Board," Tomigusuku City Board of Education, 2015. * Tomigusuku Village Board of Education, "Cultural Properties of Tomigusuku Village (Revised)," 2002, p.13. * Tomigusuku City History Editing Committee, Folk Culture Division, "Tomigusuku City History, Volume 2: Folk Culture," Tomigusuku City Hall, 2008, pp.616,628. * Tomigusuku Minwa no Kai, Kimio Isa, "Takayasu Bijun Monogatari," 1997. * Takayasu District History Editing Committee, "Takayasu-shi Vol.1," 1999, p.61.
== See also == * [[:en:Tomigusuku, Okinawa|Tomigusuku]]
== External links == * [http://www.city.tomigusuku.okinawa.jp/tourism_culture/146/2722 Cultural Property Information - Tomigusuku City Homepage] * [https://www.google.com/maps/d/u/0/viewer?hl=ja&authuser=0&mid=1okYOCSqgTnJr48vqtXJWEXfeEtM Tomigusuku City Cultural Property Information Map]

Nowadays, families individually visit the shrine to pray for good health and household safety, and it has become customary for households celebrating new births or new homes to offer bottles of {{ill|Awamori|en|Awamori|WD=Q1069948|lt=awamori}}.<ref name="city"/> Additionally, younger village officials called ''yakume'' clean the area around Bijun the day before, and on the day of the event, they gather coral stones from Senaga Island as offerings to welcome visitors.
* Other customs include village officials worshipping at the "Umanchū" shrine on {{ill|Senaga Island|ja|瀬長島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senaga Island|1=Senaga Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4518914}} the day before Bijun Hēshi until before WWII, and even today, on the following day (the 14th), clan groups ({{ill|munchū|ja|門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/munchū|1=munchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10333146}}) perform a ceremony called Shinagamunumē (also known as Anjinamunumē) on Senaga Island.<ref>{{cite book|language=ja|author=Tomigusuku Village Board of Education|title=Cultural Properties of Tomigusuku Village (Revised)|year=2002|page=13}}</ref> The exact historical relationship between Takayasu and Senaga Island is unclear, but the Bijun Hēshi period is a significant time for interaction between the people of Takayasu and Senaga Island.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高安のビジュン]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高安のビジュン]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高安のビジュン]]
'''Takayasu no Bijun''' (高安のビジュン) is a worship site dedicated to a sacred stone located in Takayasu, {{ill|Tomigusuku, Okinawa|en|Tomigusuku, Okinawa|WD=Q371446|lt=Tomigusuku}}, Okinawa Prefecture.
== Overview == <ref>Tomigusuku City Board of Education Cultural Affairs Division, "Takayasu no Bijun Cultural Property Information Board," Tomigusuku City Board of Education, 2015.</ref> * ''Bijuru'' or ''Bijun'' refers to a tradition of worshiping sacred stones, primarily found throughout the main island of Okinawa. In Tomigusuku, it is commonly known as Bijun. At Takayasu no Bijun, a single rounded sandstone called ''Nībinu-funi'' (fine-grained sandstone) is enshrined as the sacred stone.
* The following legend is associated with this Bijun: "Long ago, two youths from {{ill|Kanegusuku Magiri|ja|兼城間切|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanegusuku Magiri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Zaha (present-day Itoman City Zaha) were each carrying sacred sake intended as an offering to the king in Shuri. They stopped to rest in front of the Bijun. Tired from carrying the heavy sake, each made a request to the Bijun. One youth wished, 'Please make my sake lighter,' while the other earnestly asked, 'Please give me the strength to carry this sake.' The youth who lazily wished for lighter sake had the rope holding his sake barrel snap, spilling all of the sake. Meanwhile, the sincere youth who asked for strength safely delivered the sake to Shuri. Thus, the Bijun deity is revered as a benevolent god who grants strength to the earnest." This story is known as the Takayasu no Bijun Monogatari and was adapted into a kamishibai by the Takayasu Children's Association.<ref>{{cite book|language=ja|author1=Tomigusuku Minwa no Kai|author2=Kimio Isa|title=Takayasu Bijun Monogatari|year=1997}}</ref>
* At Takayasu no Bijun, a traditional event called Bijun Hēshi (also known as Agī Hēshi) is held annually on the 13th day of the 9th month of the lunar calendar.<ref name="city">{{cite book|language=ja|author=Tomigusuku City History Editing Committee, Folk Culture Division|title=Tomigusuku City History, Volume 2: Folk Culture|publisher=Tomigusuku City Hall|year=2008|pages=616,628}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|language=ja|author=Takayasu District History Editing Committee|title=Takayasu-shi Vol.1|year=1999|page=61}}</ref> Until around 1960, many local villagers gathered at Bijun square for festive singing accompanied by the {{ill|Sanshin|en|Sanshin|WD=Q2709639|lt=sanshin}}. Nowadays, families individually visit the shrine to pray for good health and household safety, and it has become customary for households celebrating new births or new homes to offer bottles of {{ill|Awamori|en|Awamori|WD=Q1069948|lt=awamori}}.<ref name="city"/> Additionally, younger village officials called ''yakume'' clean the area around Bijun the day before, and on the day of the event, they gather coral stones from Senaga Island as offerings to welcome visitors.
* Other customs include village officials worshipping at the "Umanchū" shrine on {{ill|Senaga Island|ja|瀬長島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senaga Island|1=Senaga Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4518914}} the day before Bijun Hēshi until before WWII, and even today, on the following day (the 14th), clan groups ({{ill|munchū|ja|門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/munchū|1=munchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10333146}}) perform a ceremony called Shinagamunumē (also known as Anjinamunumē) on Senaga Island.<ref>{{cite book|language=ja|author=Tomigusuku Village Board of Education|title=Cultural Properties of Tomigusuku Village (Revised)|year=2002|page=13}}</ref> The exact historical relationship between Takayasu and Senaga Island is unclear, but the Bijun Hēshi period is a significant time for interaction between the people of Takayasu and Senaga Island.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高安のビジュン]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高安のビジュン]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:高安のビジュン]]
'''Takayasu no Bijun''' (高安のビジュン) is a worship site dedicated to a sacred stone located in Takayasu, {{ill|Tomigusuku, Okinawa|en|Tomigusuku, Okinawa|WD=Q371446|lt=Tomigusuku|1=Tomigusuku, Okinawa}}, Okinawa Prefecture.
== Overview == <ref>Tomigusuku City Board of Education Cultural Affairs Division, "Takayasu no Bijun Cultural Property Information Board," Tomigusuku City Board of Education, 2015.</ref> * ''Bijuru'' or ''Bijun'' refers to a tradition of worshiping sacred stones, primarily found throughout the main island of Okinawa. In Tomigusuku, it is commonly known as Bijun. At Takayasu no Bijun, a single rounded sandstone called ''Nībinu-funi'' (fine-grained sandstone) is enshrined as the sacred stone.
* The following legend is associated with this Bijun: "Long ago, two youths from {{ill|Kanegusuku Magiri|ja|兼城間切|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kanegusuku Magiri|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} Zaha (present-day Itoman City Zaha) were each carrying sacred sake intended as an offering to the king in Shuri. They stopped to rest in front of the Bijun. Tired from carrying the heavy sake, each made a request to the Bijun. One youth wished, 'Please make my sake lighter,' while the other earnestly asked, 'Please give me the strength to carry this sake.' The youth who lazily wished for lighter sake had the rope holding his sake barrel snap, spilling all of the sake. Meanwhile, the sincere youth who asked for strength safely delivered the sake to Shuri. Thus, the Bijun deity is revered as a benevolent god who grants strength to the earnest." This story is known as the Takayasu no Bijun Monogatari and was adapted into a kamishibai by the Takayasu Children's Association.<ref>{{cite book|language=ja|author1=Tomigusuku Minwa no Kai|author2=Kimio Isa|title=Takayasu Bijun Monogatari|year=1997}}</ref>
* At Takayasu no Bijun, a traditional event called Bijun Hēshi (also known as Agī Hēshi) is held annually on the 13th day of the 9th month of the lunar calendar.<ref name="city">{{cite book|language=ja|author=Tomigusuku City History Editing Committee, Folk Culture Division|title=Tomigusuku City History, Volume 2: Folk Culture|publisher=Tomigusuku City Hall|year=2008|pages=616,628}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|language=ja|author=Takayasu District History Editing Committee|title=Takayasu-shi Vol.1|year=1999|page=61}}</ref> Until around 1960, many local villagers gathered at Bijun square for festive singing accompanied by the {{ill|Sanshin|en|Sanshin|WD=Q2709639|lt=sanshin|1=Sanshin}}. Nowadays, families individually visit the shrine to pray for good health and household safety, and it has become customary for households celebrating new births or new homes to offer bottles of {{ill|Awamori|en|Awamori|WD=Q1069948|lt=awamori|1=Awamori}}.<ref name="city"/> Additionally, younger village officials called ''yakume'' clean the area around Bijun the day before, and on the day of the event, they gather coral stones from Senaga Island as offerings to welcome visitors.
* Other customs include village officials worshipping at the "Umanchū" shrine on {{ill|Senaga Island|ja|瀬長島|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Senaga Island|1=Senaga Island|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q4518914}} the day before Bijun Hēshi until before WWII, and even today, on the following day (the 14th), clan groups ({{ill|munchū|ja|門中|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/munchū|1=munchū|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10333146}}) perform a ceremony called Shinagamunumē (also known as Anjinamunumē) on Senaga Island.<ref>{{cite book|language=ja|author=Tomigusuku Village Board of Education|title=Cultural Properties of Tomigusuku Village (Revised)|year=2002|page=13}}</ref> The exact historical relationship between Takayasu and Senaga Island is unclear, but the Bijun Hēshi period is a significant time for interaction between the people of Takayasu and Senaga Island.
